items.q: What was the Elizabethan Religious Settlement of 1559?
items.correct: A deliberate via media — a Protestant doctrine and English-language prayer book combined with some Catholic-style ceremony — designed to maximise conformity.
items.misconception: Elizabeth made England fully Protestant and banned Catholic worship outright.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth reunited England with the Pope to make peace with Spain.
items.why: The settlement was a compromise designed to be broadly acceptable. Students assume binary Protestant/Catholic divides; Elizabeth deliberately blurred them to keep her realm stable.

items.q: Why did Prohibition fail in the United States?
items.correct: Widespread evasion via speakeasies and bootlegging, growth of organised crime, loss of tax revenue, and changing public opinion led to repeal in 1933.
items.misconception: Prohibition failed because most Americans were already alcoholics.
items.random_wrong: Prohibition failed because the President personally banned the police from enforcing it.
items.why: Most Americans drank moderately, not pathologically. Failure came from impracticality of enforcement and organised crime profits — a regulatory story, not a moral one.

items.q: Why did Britain pursue appeasement in the 1930s?
items.correct: Britain was militarily unprepared, traumatised by WWI losses, sympathetic to revising Versailles, and feared communism more than Nazism.
items.misconception: Because Chamberlain was a coward and admired Hitler.
items.random_wrong: Because Britain had no army at all in the 1930s.
items.why: Hindsight makes appeasement look like weakness. In context it was a rational (if mistaken) strategy reflecting public opinion, financial constraints and genuine fear of another total war.
It's King John. “Richard I” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Runnymede. “London Tower” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Magna Carta sealed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1215. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
